medications.indication_rationale: Oral tetracycline is first-line systemic therapy for moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne, reducing Cutibacterium acnes load and, importantly, dermal inflammation — arresting the flare and reducing scarring risk. Prescribing requires a prescriber (telehealth or same-day GP).
medications.dose_route_frequency: doxycycline 100 mg orally once daily (as stated in the source note)
medications.duration: typically several weeks, reviewed at follow-up
contraindications_in_this_case: Pregnancy and breastfeeding — doxycycline is contraindicated; pregnancy status must be checked in a person of child-bearing potential before prescribing (flagged: patient sex/age inferred).
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Counsel to separate dosing from the benzoyl peroxide routine's other topicals and from antacids/iron/dairy which reduce absorption; take with a large glass of water, sitting upright, to avoid oesophageal irritation.

medications.indication_rationale: Topical antibiotic adjunct for inflammatory lesions; best combined with benzoyl peroxide to limit antibiotic resistance.
medications.dose_route_frequency: clindamycin 1% lotion applied topically to affected areas (as stated in the source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the acute local pain, taken with food as needed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally with food, as needed (PRN) (as stated in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Active peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment or NSAID hypersensitivity — none reported

medications.drug_name: empirical intravenous / broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed facial cellulitis
medications.indication_rationale: The red-flag screen for spreading cellulitis is negative (non-advancing erythema, systemically well, no periorbital signs). Treating this as facial cellulitis with IV/broad-spectrum antibiotics or an ED admission is over-management driven by the anchoring trap. It becomes appropriate ONLY if the safety-net cellulitis triggers develop.

Isotretinoin is specialist-initiated with mandatory pregnancy-prevention and monitoring; it is not an acute-flare treatment and must not be started remotely via telehealth. Referral for consideration is a later, non-acute step.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a clean warm compress to the painful lumps for about 10 minutes in the evening to ease pressure; do NOT squeeze, pop or lance the cysts.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Warm compresses relieve pressure and encourage natural drainage; manual squeezing worsens inflammation, infection and scarring.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the doxycycline with a large glass of water, remain upright for 30 minutes, and use the benzoyl peroxide/topicals consistently rather than intermittently.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents oesophageal irritation and improves treatment response; inconsistent topical use contributed to the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge the psychosocial impact and offer GP mental-health follow-up given the distress and social withdrawal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Severe acne carries significant psychological morbidity that should be addressed alongside the skin.

patient_education_points.point: This is a severe flare of an inflammatory skin condition, not a spreading skin infection — the oral and topical antibiotics work by calming the deep inflammation and reducing the bacteria in the cysts, so the acute pain and weeping should ease over 48-72 hours.
The underlying skin condition takes weeks to months of consistent treatment to settle; getting on top of it promptly also reduces the risk of permanent scarring.

Watch the redness: if it starts spreading across the cheek with a firm advancing edge, the eyelid swells, you get a fever or shakes, or any eye pain or change in vision, seek in-person or emergency care straight away.
Do not squeeze or pop the cysts, as this worsens scarring and infection risk.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review in about 7 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response to the oral antibiotic, resolution of the acute inflammation, and a plan for longer-term acne management including possible dermatology referral for nodulocystic disease.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness with an advancing edge, eyelid swelling, fever/rigors, any eye pain or visual change, or pain uncontrolled by simple analgesia — bring the review forward or escalate per the safety-net.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a severe inflammatory flare of a chronic skin condition rather than a spreading facial cellulitis, having screened the spread pattern and systemic/periorbital features
Arrange a prescriber (telehealth or same-day GP) for oral antibiotic + topical therapy
Advise simple analgesia for the acute pain
Give explicit cellulitis safety-netting: same-day in-person review for spreading/advancing redness or eyelid swelling, and emergency care for fever/rigors or any eye pain/visual change
Advise against squeezing or popping the cysts
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a same-day/next-day in-person GP review to lay eyes on the lesions and prescribe is acceptable given telehealth cannot palpate — provided it is not escalated to ED/000.
Deferring the specific antibiotic choice to the prescriber while clearly indicating systemic + topical therapy is needed.
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for spreading facial/periorbital cellulitis
Failing to arrange a prescriber for systemic therapy
Failing to advise analgesia for severe pain
errors_of_commission: Escalating to ED or calling 000 for presumed facial cellulitis despite a negative red-flag screen (over-triage)
Prescribing/recommending empirical IV or broad-spectrum antibiotics for cellulitis that is not present
Advising the patient to squeeze, pop or lance the cysts at home
Over-investigating a systemically-well patient with bloods/inflammatory markers
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass recognises the benign chronic cause, arranges appropriate systemic + topical therapy with analgesia, and attaches prominent cellulitis/periorbital safety-netting. Escalating to ED/000 for a cellulitis the screen argues against is the primary scored over-triage failure; dismissing it as trivial self-care with no prescriber and no safety-net is the primary under-triage failure.
